structure.description: Catalog has a tri-state primary CTA per item: Browse (not installed) → Install (creates instance) → Enable (replace .isEnabled true) → Disable (replace .isEnabled false). State machine encoded in CTA label and disabled-state, not in separate forms. Optional 'Uninstall' is exposed as secondary destructive action with irreversibility flag.

rationale.hypothesis: Marketplaces have a non-obvious lifecycle: install ≠ enable. Conflating them (one button → installed-and-on) is convenient but wrong for plugins-with-side-effects (auth, network access) where enable is the consent moment. Splitting into install + enable + disable + uninstall as a 4-state CTA makes consent surfaces explicit, lets users sandbox-install before turning on, and makes 'why is this plugin running?' answerable.

evidence.description: Community Plugins: Browse → Install → Enable, with separate toggle in installed list
Install button → installed; per-extension Enable/Disable/Reload toggle in extensions list
Add to Chrome (install+enable atomic), but per-extension on/off toggle in chrome://extensions — same conceptual lifecycle

counterexample.description: Install = enabled by default for mobile apps; the install/enable split would be friction without security benefit at user level

shouldMatch.reason: Integrations have install + enable as distinct moments

shouldNotMatch.reason: Buying ≠ enabling; lifecycle is purchase-driven, not capability-driven
